mcqs.question: Which of the following is the correct syntax to declare a class in C++?
mcqs.explanation: In C++, the keyword 'class' is used (not 'Class'), and the class body ends with a semicolon after the closing brace.

mcqs.question: What is the default access specifier for members of a class in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Class members are private by default in C++, meaning they cannot be accessed from outside the class without getter/setter functions.
The destructor is automatically called when the object t goes out of scope at the end of main(), printing 'Destructor'.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the 'this' pointer in C++?

options.text: To point to the next object in memory
To access the current object's own members
To allocate memory for the object

mcqs.explanation: The 'this' pointer is an implicit parameter to all member functions and points to the current object invoking the function.

mcqs.question: How many copies of a static data member exist for a class with multiple objects?

options.text: One copy shared by all objects of the class
Zero copies until an object is created
One copy per member function call

mcqs.explanation: Static data members are class-level variables shared by all objects of the class; only one copy exists regardless of the number of objects.

mcqs.question: Which statement about friend functions in C++ is TRUE?

options.text: Friend functions are members of the class
Friend functions can access private members without being members
Friend functions cannot be declared in the private section
Friend functions are inherited by derived classes

mcqs.explanation: Friend functions are non-member functions that are granted special access to private and protected members of a class.

options.text: Compilation error - x is not accessible
0 - accessing protected member through derived class
Compilation error - x is not initialized

mcqs.explanation: Protected members are accessible within derived classes through inheritance, so the code compiles and prints the value of x (default-initialized to 0 for int).
Each constructor increments the static count and assigns it to id. After creating 3 objects, count is 3. c1.show() prints 1, c2.show() prints 2, and Counter::display() prints 3.
